title: Dyeing with black bean water
subtitle: A cold vat dye using black bean soaking water to produce blue-grey tones on wool and silk
excerpt: Black bean soaking water is a cold dye that produces blue-grey tones on protein fibres without heat or a mordant. The colour comes from anthocyanin pigments that are destroyed by heat, which is why the process uses cold water only. The colour is not lightfast and fades with washing, but it is one of the most accessible natural dyeing experiments available.
sourceNotes: Black bean water as a dye is documented in Sasha Duerr's 'The Handbook of Natural Plant Dyes' and in UK natural dye instruction. Anthocyanin chemistry and pH sensitivity are consistent across dye chemistry references. Observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dye bath that operates at room temperature, relying on prolonged soaking rather than heat to transfer colour to the fibre. Black bean water is used as a cold vat because the anthocyanin pigments that produce the blue-grey colour are destroyed by heat above approximately 50°C.

A group of water-soluble plant pigments that produce blue, purple, and red tones. Found in black beans, red cabbage, elderberries, and many other plants. Anthocyanin dyes have low lightfastness and shift colour with pH: the same pigment appears blue-grey at neutral pH, purple in acid, and green in alkali.

A change in the colour of an anthocyanin dye caused by adjusting the acidity or alkalinity of the dye bath. Adding a small amount of white wine vinegar (acid) to black bean water shifts it toward purple; adding bicarbonate of soda (alkali) shifts it toward green-grey. pH shifts allow several colours from one dye bath.

content.text: dye derived from soaking dried black beans overnight. The blue-grey colour comes from
in the bean skins. These pigments are highly sensitive to

, which makes it possible to produce several different colours from a single batch of bean water. The beans themselves can be drained and cooked as normal after soaking. The Herbal Apothecary category includes black beans as a nutritious culinary legume if you want to explore their food uses alongside the dyeing.

attrs.title: Equipment safety
attrs.body: Use separate containers for natural dyeing. Never use containers or utensils for food after they have held dye materials.

items.name: Wool or silk (pre-wetted in cool water), dry weight
White wine vinegar for acid pH shift (optional)
Bicarbonate of soda for alkaline pH shift (optional)
Large bowl or container, designated for dyeing

content.text: Cover the black beans with 2 litres of cold water and leave to soak for 8 to 12 hours (overnight). The water will turn deep blue-black. Drain the beans and reserve the soaking water; the beans can now be cooked and eaten as normal.

Submerge the pre-wetted wool or silk in the cold bean water. Leave to soak at room temperature for 4 to 8 hours, turning the fibre occasionally so all surfaces have equal contact with the dye. Do not heat the dye bath: temperatures above 50°C destroy the anthocyanin pigments and the colour will be lost. The fibre will appear deeply coloured in the bath but will lighten significantly on drying.
Step 3: pH shifts for colour variation

While the fibre is soaking, divide the remaining bean water into smaller containers. Add a small amount of white wine vinegar to one (the liquid will shift toward purple) and a pinch of bicarbonate of soda to another (the liquid will shift toward green-grey). Dip small skeins into the shifted baths to produce a range of tones from the same bean water. Rinse the fibre after pH modification to stabilise the colour.

Rinse the dyed fibre in cool water until the water runs clear. Squeeze gently and hang to dry away from direct sunlight. The colour will be noticeably lighter when dry. Keep black bean-dyed items away from strong light and wash in cool water to slow fading.

items.problem: The colour dries to near-white with almost no blue-grey remaining
items.cause: The bean soaking water was too dilute (too little water removed too much pigment), or the soaking time for the fibre was too short
items.fix: Use less water for soaking the beans to produce a more concentrated liquid. Increase the fibre soaking time to 8 or more hours and ensure the fibre remains fully submerged throughout.

items.problem: The colour faded completely after the first wash
items.cause: Anthocyanin dyes have inherently low washfastness and the wash temperature was too warm
items.fix: Always wash black bean-dyed items in cold water (under 20°C) with no detergent. If soap is needed, use a small amount of pH-neutral wash. Anthocyanin is the most fugitive common natural dye. Expect decorative colour that fades over time, not permanent results.
